claim: Riluzole extends survival by 2-3 months in ALS, the first drug proven to modify ALS disease course
notes: Bensimon et al 1994 (n=155) and Lacomblez et al 1996 (n=959). Two landmark RCTs establishing riluzole as first ALS therapy. 100mg/day optimal. Mechanism: glutamate release inhibition via sodium channel blockade + EAAT2 upregulation.

claim: Riluzole provides only modest survival benefit (2-3 months) with limited functional impact, highlighting the need for combination approaches
notes: Miller et al 2007, Cochrane review. Riluzole extends median tracheostomy-free survival by ~2-3 months. No significant effect on muscle strength or motor function. Sets the baseline — any future therapy must improve upon this.

claim: EAAT2/GLT-1 glutamate transporter is markedly reduced in ALS spinal cord and motor cortex, causing excitotoxic glutamate accumulation
notes: Rothstein et al 1992, 1995. Loss of EAAT2 in ALS tissue leads to impaired synaptic glutamate clearance. Provides mechanistic rationale for riluzole and other anti-excitotoxic approaches. EAAT2 loss may be consequence of TDP-43 pathology.

claim: Aberrant EAAT2 mRNA processing produces non-functional splice variants in ALS tissue, explaining EAAT2 protein loss
notes: Lin et al 1998, Neuron. Aberrant splicing of EAAT2 mRNA in ALS. Non-functional truncated protein produced. May be upstream consequence of RNA processing disruption (TDP-43 pathology?).

claim: Masitinib add-on to riluzole showed survival benefit in normal-progressor subgroup of ALS patients in Phase 2/3 trial
notes: Mora et al 2020, 2021. Masitinib 4.5mg/kg/day. Normal progressors (baseline ALSFRS-R slope > -1.1/mo) showed significant benefit. Mechanism: tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting CSF1R (microglia) and KIT (mast cells).

claim: Motor neuron onset is determined by mutant SOD1 within MNs, but disease progression is driven by mutant SOD1 in surrounding glia (non-cell-autonomous toxicity)
notes: Boillee et al 2006, Science. Chimeric mice with selective SOD1 expression. Landmark paper establishing non-cell-autonomous mechanism. Microglia and astrocytes with mutant SOD1 drive MN death. Foundation for glial-targeting therapies.

claim: Astrocytes bearing ALS mutations are selectively toxic to motor neurons through secreted factors
notes: Di Giorgio et al 2008, Nagai et al 2007. Co-culture experiments showing mutant astrocytes kill wild-type MNs through secreted toxic factor(s). Mechanism partially identified: involves prostaglandin D2, inflammatory mediators.

claim: Zilucoplan C5 complement inhibition did not meet primary endpoint in HEALEY ALS Platform Trial
notes: HEALEY ALS Platform Trial Regimen C. Terminal complement pathway inhibition alone insufficient to slow ALS progression. Complement activation may be secondary to upstream pathology.

claim: Cortical hyperexcitability is a presymptomatic feature of ALS, present in SOD1 carriers before symptom onset
notes: Vucic et al 2008, Brain. Threshold tracking TMS in presymptomatic SOD1 carriers. Cortical hyperexcitability precedes clinical onset. Supports excitotoxicity as early pathogenic mechanism, not just consequence.

claim: iPSC-derived motor neurons from ALS patients show intrinsic hyperexcitability, validating excitotoxicity as cell-autonomous
notes: Wainger et al 2014, Cell Reports. iPSC MNs from SOD1 and C9orf72 ALS patients showed hyperexcitability. Retigabine (Kv7 opener) reduced hyperexcitability in vitro. Led to clinical testing.

claim: Retigabine (Kv7 channel opener) reduces motor neuron hyperexcitability in iPSC models and was tested in ALS patients
notes: Wainger et al 2014, 2021. iPSC data showed hyperexcitability rescue. Phase 2 trial showed target engagement (reduced excitability) but no clear ALSFRS-R benefit at tested doses/duration. Proof of concept for excitability targeting.

claim: Ibudilast shows trends toward slowing ALSFRS-R decline and FVC loss through PDE-mediated neuroinflammation reduction in ALS
notes: IBU-ALS Phase 2 trial. PDE inhibitor with anti-inflammatory properties. Trends in ALSFRS-R and FVC but not statistically significant at n~230. Multi-target: PDE4, PDE10, MIF inhibition.

claim: Perampanel AMPA receptor antagonist shows preliminary signals of benefit in ALS case series targeting cortical hyperexcitability
notes: Aizawa et al 2018. Small case series (n=8) of perampanel in ALS. Some patients showed ALSFRS-R stabilization. Rationale: AMPA-mediated excitotoxicity is a driver of MN death. Complementary to riluzole (which targets glutamate release).

claim: Verdiperstat MPO inhibitor did not meet primary or secondary endpoints in HEALEY ALS Platform Trial
notes: HEALEY ALS Platform Trial Regimen B. Myeloperoxidase inhibition alone insufficient. Oxidative damage from MPO may be too downstream or too narrow a target for meaningful ALS modification.

claim: DNL343 eIF2B activator did not meet primary endpoint in HEALEY ALS Platform Trial
notes: HEALEY ALS Platform Trial Regimen G. ISR modulation via eIF2B activation insufficient as monotherapy. Translation regulation likely not the rate-limiting step in established ALS.

claim: AMX0035/Relyvrio failed to show efficacy in PHOENIX Phase 3 confirmatory trial, leading to voluntary market withdrawal
notes: PHOENIX Phase 3, n=664. Primary endpoint (ALSFRS-R slope) not met. Original CENTAUR Phase 2 benefit not reproduced. Amylyx voluntarily withdrew Relyvrio from market April 2024. Cautionary tale for accelerated approval.

claim: Edaravone showed ALSFRS-R benefit in a narrowly defined subgroup (definite/probable ALS, FVC>=80%, 2yr onset) but generalizability remains debated
notes: Writing Group 2017, Lancet Neurology. Study 19 (positive) used enriched population. Broader studies (Study 16) negative. Raises questions about which ALS patients benefit from antioxidant approach.

claim: CNM-Au8 gold nanocrystals failed primary endpoint in RESCUE-ALS but showed trends in secondary biomarker endpoints
notes: RESCUE-ALS Phase 2 trial. Primary endpoint (ALSFRS-R) not met. Some biomarker signals (NAD+ levels). HEALEY platform also tested. Bioenergetic enhancement alone may be insufficient.

claim: Microglial activation correlates with disease progression in ALS and transitions from neuroprotective M2 to neurotoxic M1 phenotype
notes: Brettschneider et al 2012, Henkel et al 2009. Early M2 microglia are neuroprotective; progressive conversion to M1 drives neuroinflammatory MN death. Timing of anti-inflammatory intervention may be critical.
